title: The Greenspan Lesson for the AI Race
gist: CSIS's Karl Smith argues the Fed should replay Alan Greenspan's 1990s bet — trusting a hidden productivity surge over backward-looking statistics — because that earlier bet built the cloud, chip, and research infrastructure underpinning today's AI dominance (the Magnificent Seven now exceed the EU and Japan's combined market cap), and a premature rate hike now risks choking the trillion-dollar AI buildout just as Silicon Valley's self-financing era ends.

insights.claim: Outpacing China in the AI race will require U.S. corporations to borrow trillions of dollars to finance data centers, power generation and transmission, autonomous-vehicle factories, and daily low-Earth-orbit satellite launches.
insights.speaker: Karl Smith
insights.so_what: Frames the AI race as a capital-intensive megacycle financed by corporate debt — Fed rate policy becomes a direct lever on the pace of the AI buildout, a macro risk factor for AI-infrastructure investors.
insights.quote: It will require U.S. corporations to borrow trillions of dollars to finance the massive physical capital investment required to outpace China.
insights.locator: Winning the AI race against China

insights.claim: Official U.S. statistics currently register AI-related capital investment as rising input costs rather than as a down payment on future efficiency gains, risking a Federal Reserve that inadvertently stifles the AI productivity revolution.
insights.speaker: Karl Smith
insights.so_what: If the Fed reads AI capex as inflationary rather than productivity-enhancing, it risks tightening prematurely and choking the buildout — watch Fed rhetoric on how it frames capex versus inflation.
insights.quote: official statistics register all that investment as an increase in input costs, rather than as a down payment on massive efficiency gains.
insights.locator: Deft monetary policy plays a crucial role

insights.claim: In 1992 the Taylor rule said interest rates were about right, but Alan Greenspan judged the 'jobless recovery' economy was far from full employment and kept cutting rates, betting that official productivity data understated a hidden IT-driven productivity gain — echoing Robert Solow's quip that computers were visible everywhere except in the productivity statistics.
insights.speaker: Karl Smith
insights.so_what: Establishes the reusable mental model: trust ground-level signals of a structural productivity shift over lagging official statistics when the two conflict — the analogy the author applies directly to AI today.
insights.quote: computers were visible everywhere except in the productivity statistics
insights.locator: Even more important, Greenspan took seriously Bob Solow's famous quip

insights.claim: In 1992, only 10% of Americans described the U.S. economy as good or excellent versus 90% who called it fair or poor — the worst reading ever recorded outside a recession — even as the Taylor rule implied policy was already appropriate.
insights.speaker: Karl Smith
insights.so_what: Illustrates how a sentiment/hard-data divergence preceded a policy inflection point — a template for spotting mispriced macro turning points.
insights.quote: Only 10 percent of Americans described the economy as good or excellent, compared with 90 percent who called it fair or poor.

insights.claim: Greenspan resisted tightening sharply even after unemployment fell below the 6% rate economists then considered the sustainable 'natural rate' by September 1994, and the feared inflation breakout never materialized.
insights.speaker: Karl Smith
insights.so_what: Shows the payoff of not tightening on a stale 'natural rate' assumption — a precedent for arguing the Fed should tolerate below-consensus unemployment or above-target readings during a tech buildout.

insights.claim: U.S. productivity growth accelerated from about 1.5% per year (1990-95) to above 2% in 1996 and as high as 4.2% by 1999, while unemployment fell to 3.9%, far below the rate economists had assumed was sustainable.
insights.speaker: Karl Smith
insights.so_what: The realized outcome that validated Greenspan's bet — a quantitative benchmark for how large a 'hidden' productivity surge can turn out to be.
insights.quote: By 1999, productivity growth reached as high as 4.2 percent.
insights.locator: Then the economy began to do what Greenspan suspected

insights.claim: Despite waste like Pets.com, the late-1990s investment boom left the U.S. economy 14% larger by 2003 than it would have been without the productivity surge (average 3.15%/year growth from 1996-2003), surviving both the dot-com crash and the post-9/11 recession.
insights.speaker: Karl Smith
insights.so_what: Counters the 'bubble equals wasted capital' narrative — even a boom with visible excess can leave a durable, GDP-scale productivity dividend, an analogy for evaluating today's AI capex.
insights.quote: the U.S. economy was 14 percent larger by 2003 than it would have been without the productivity surge.
insights.locator: First, note that despite both the dot-com crash

insights.claim: The Magnificent Seven stocks (Alphabet, Apple, Meta, Nvidia, Amazon, Microsoft, Tesla) account for a third of the S&P 500's market capitalization and alone exceed the combined market capitalization of the European Union and Japan.
insights.speaker: Karl Smith
insights.so_what: Quantifies how much of today's U.S. equity market value traces back to the dot-com-era investment cycle Greenspan enabled — a scale reference for how large an AI-driven cycle could compound into.
insights.quote: Those seven companies alone exceed the combined market capitalization of the European Union and Japan.

insights.claim: The dot-com boom built the specific infrastructure underpinning U.S. AI dominance — Microsoft and Amazon's cloud platforms, Google's research culture that produced the transformer, Nvidia's chips, Meta's open-source push, and Apple's device distribution — with OpenAI scaling through Microsoft's cloud/capital and Anthropic growing on the same chip, cloud, venture-capital, and Bay Area talent base.
insights.speaker: Karl Smith
insights.so_what: Maps the direct lineage from 1990s capex winners to today's AI value chain — informs which incumbents (cloud/chips/distribution) capture durable AI rents versus which labs remain dependent on them.
insights.quote: The dot-com boom created the operating system for U.S. AI dominance.
insights.locator: Even the new AI labs grew up around that core.

insights.claim: Unlike the 1990s, where falling unemployment triggered false inflation fears, today the U.S. has stubbornly high inflation stuck above the Fed's 2% target with the Fed biased toward raising rates — inverting the Greenspan setup and raising the risk of tightening into an unmeasured AI productivity surge.
insights.speaker: Karl Smith
insights.so_what: The key asymmetry to track: this cycle's risk is a rate hike (not just a missed cut) choking AI capex — a direct read-through to AI-infrastructure equity and credit exposure.
insights.quote: we have stubbornly high inflation that seems stuck above the Fed’s 2 percent target. And the Fed seems biased toward raising rates.
insights.locator: The United States now face a similar position.

insights.claim: The price of a given level of AI performance has fallen ninefold to nine-hundredfold per year according to the best available analysis, a deflation rate current cost/productivity statistics almost certainly fail to capture.
insights.speaker: Karl Smith
insights.so_what: A concrete deflation benchmark for AI compute/inference cost curves — useful for modeling unit economics of AI-native software companies.
insights.quote: the price of a given level of AI performance has fallen by ninefold to nine-hundredfold per year.
insights.locator: Moreover, the cost of AI capabilities is dropping

insights.claim: Silicon Valley's ability to self-finance the AI buildout will likely end sometime in 2026, shifting the sector toward debt financing whose cost will hinge directly on where the Federal Reserve sets interest rates.
insights.speaker: Karl Smith
insights.so_what: Signals an imminent shift from equity/cash-financed AI capex to debt-financed capex — raises the sensitivity of AI infrastructure spending, and related credit/equity, to Fed rate moves later in 2026.
insights.quote: That era will likely come to an end sometime this year.
insights.locator: Until now, Silicon Valley has been able to mostly self-finance
